title: QA: test output schema strictness — verify extra fields don't break Claude Code
description: During QA phase, test whether .strict() vs .passthrough() on hook stdout schemas matters. Send output with extra fields to Claude Code and verify behavior. If Claude Code ignores extras, passthrough is fine. If it rejects them, switch to strict. Deferred from Story 4.1 (ch-6rb).
notes:
## Resolved doubts (2026-03-07)

### Probe mechanism
Use probe-launcher scripts. Usage guide tracked in separate issue (ch-1154). Agent writes a new probe script that sends hook stdout with extra JSON fields, observes Claude Code behavior.

### Extra fields to test
- hookwatch_version: '0.1.0' (primary test)
- debug: true (harmless secondary test)
Add as named probe scripts in scripts/claude-code-probes/.

### Zod context
.passthrough() preserves unrecognized keys in parsed output; defined fields validate normally. .strict() throws on unrecognized keys. Question is whether Claude Code's consumer side rejects extra fields in hook stdout.

### Scripts mapping
All probe scripts must have package.json script entries. Tracked separately (ch-1154).

### Runtime dependency
Agent should try running the probe against real Claude Code. If claude CLI is not available or probe fails, report back with findings so we can discuss.

### Result interpretation
If Claude Code rejects extra fields → switch from .passthrough() to default z.object() (strip extras before sending). Document findings as a small section in docs/hook-execution-cheatsheet.md.

### Version recording
Record the Claude Code version tested (claude --version) in the probe output/report.
close_reason: .passthrough() confirmed safe — Claude Code 2.1.71 accepts extra fields in hook stdout. Probe script + cheat sheet section added. Commit: 0610d91

title: Research: Claude Code behavior when hook process is signal-killed
description:
Empirical test: what does Claude Code do when a hook process is terminated by signal (SIGKILL/SIGTERM) instead of exiting normally?

Motivation: hookwatch wraps hook execution. When the wrapped command is killed by signal, hookwatch must decide what exit code to forward. We should replicate Claude Code's default behavior, not invent our own. This behavior is undocumented.

Test plan:
1. Create a temporary workspace with a minimal Claude Code plugin
2. Hook does: kill -9 $$ (self-SIGKILL on one event type)
3. Run under Claude Code, observe: what does Claude Code display? Error? Non-blocking? Crash?
4. Also test SIGTERM (kill -15 $$) — may differ from SIGKILL
5. Test with exit 1 for comparison (documented as 'non-blocking error, stderr in verbose mode')

Output:
- Document findings in docs/research/signal-kill-behavior.md
- Update docs/hook-stdout-schema.md with signal behavior section
- Add signal-kill entry to cheat sheet (ch-xzia) backlog

Blocks ch-qddi (exit code on signal kill) and ch-w2pd (wrapped assertion needs to know expected behavior).
close_reason: Research complete. Findings: (1) SIGKILL/SIGTERM/exit-1 all produce identical 'hook error' from Claude Code — no differentiation. (2) Exit 0: JSON parsed; Exit 2: JSON ignored, stderr only; Other: non-blocking. (3) Node/Bun gives null exitCode + signal name for signal deaths. (4) Design decision: 128+signal convention, always exit 0 for hookwatch errors. Findings captured in ch-qddi (signal mapping) and ch-cen0 (docs). --debug run unnecessary — design decisions don't depend on exact exit codes.

title: Type design improvements: EventPostPayload discriminated union + parseHookEvent schema map
description:
Type design improvements identified by analyzer:

1. EventPostPayload bare/wrapped mode not modeled: 5 fields have different semantics by mode but the flat interface permits nonsensical combinations. Consider discriminated union with mode field. Also port belongs to transport, not payload — separate parameter.

2. parseHookEvent switch statement (18 cases) should be a schema map for easier maintenance. Replace switch with Record<string, ZodType> lookup. Prevents drift between union type and parser.

3. Extract EVENT_NAMES const array from 18 literals. Derive KnownEventName type. Use for EventRow.event field instead of plain string.

4. postEvent() manual camelCase→snake_case wire format mapping is fragile — if EventPostPayload adds a field but mapping isn't updated, field is silently dropped.

These are design improvements, not bugs. Low priority.

Sources: type-design-analyzer (types #1, #7, cross-cutting #2, #4)

title: postEvent camelCase→snake_case wire mapping: automate or type-safe
description:
postEvent() buildRequestBody manually maps camelCase→snake_case (e.g. wrappedCommand → wrapped_command). If a field is added to WrappedEventPayload but the mapping isn't updated, the field is silently dropped.

Fix: Replace manual field-by-field assignment with a typed const record mapping camelCase keys to snake_case keys. TypeScript compiler errors if a key from WrappedEventPayload is missing from the mapping.

Approach (compile-time check via typed dictionary):
1. Define a const record: camelCase key → snake_case key for all WrappedEventPayload-specific fields (wrappedCommand, stdout, stderr, exitCode, hookwatchLog)
2. Type the record so that its keys must cover all WrappedEventPayload-specific fields (using Exclude<keyof WrappedEventPayload, keyof BaseEventPayload | 'mode'> or similar)
3. buildRequestBody iterates the record to build the wire body — no more manual field assignments
4. If a new field is added to WrappedEventPayload, the compiler will error until the mapping record is updated

Keep fields that are already snake_case in the interface (if any) mapped to themselves. The mapping record is the single source of truth.

Agent: handler.
close_reason: WRAPPED_FIELD_MAP typed record with compile-time key check. Commit: fa25ac9 (merge: e78e8a9)

title: Story 3.2: Wrap I/O Viewer in Web UI
description:
As a hook author, I want to see captured wrap I/O in the web UI, so that I can debug my hook's input/output behavior visually.

## Design

There is NO separate "Wrap" event type. Wrapped events use the same event types as bare handler events (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, etc.). The distinction is the `wrapped_command` column in the DB:
- `wrapped_command` IS NULL → bare handler event
- `wrapped_command` IS NOT NULL → wrapped event

The payload for wrapped events includes: `stdout` (string), `stderr` (string), `exit_code` (number), plus the standard event fields.

### Visual distinction in event list
- Bare handler events (wrapped_command IS NULL): hollow/empty border style (outline-only rounded rectangle)
- Wrapped events (wrapped_command IS NOT NULL): solid style (filled rounded rectangle)

### Wrap-viewer component
Shows: wrapped command text, stdout panel, stderr panel, exit code (green=0, red=non-zero). Rendered when the event has a non-null wrapped_command field.

title: Story 1.6: Plugin Manifest & CLI Install/Uninstall
description:
As a Claude Code developer, I want hookwatch to work as a Claude Code plugin with proper manifest files and a CLI, so that it can be installed, managed, and invoked as both a hook handler and a standalone tool.

## Final CLI Design

```text
hookwatch install [--dry-run/-n]       # install/upgrade plugin
hookwatch uninstall [--dry-run/-n]     # uninstall + cleanup
hookwatch ui                           # start server + open browser
hookwatch --version / -v               # print version
hookwatch --help / -h                  # help

# PascalCase event family (18 registered):
hookwatch PreToolUse                   # handler mode (no args → read stdin)
hookwatch PreToolUse ./hook.sh         # wrap mode (has path → pipe + capture)
hookwatch SessionStart                 # handler mode
...etc for all 18 event types

# Unknown events: exit 1 + guide to file issue
```

## Plugin structure

```text
hookwatch/
  .claude-plugin/
    plugin.json        — GENERATED by scripts/generate-plugin.ts
  hooks/
    hooks.json         — GENERATED by scripts/generate-plugin.ts
    handler.ts         — thin entry point importing src/handler/index.ts
```

- plugin.json and hooks.json are GENERATED (not static) — version synced from package.json
- hooks.json references binary form: `"command": "hookwatch <EventType>"`
- handler.ts is thin entry point for dev mode (`cc --plugin-dir`)

## CLI (citty)

- 3 lowercase subcommands: install, uninstall, ui
- 18 PascalCase event family subcommands (handler mode only in 1.6; wrap mode added in 3.1)
- Global flags: --version/-v, --help/-h
- install/uninstall flags: --dry-run/-n
- bin field in package.json

## Key files

- scripts/generate-plugin.ts — dev tool generating plugin.json + hooks.json
- hooks/handler.ts — thin wrapper importing src/handler/index.ts
- src/cli/index.ts — citty main with all subcommands
- src/cli/install.ts — generate plugin files + install
- src/cli/uninstall.ts — uninstall + cleanup
- src/cli/ui.ts — start server + open browser

## NOT in scope for 1.6

- Wrap mode (positional arg) — that's Story 3.1
- Event subtypes/matchers — part of stdin JSON, no CLI handling needed
notes:
Resolved doubts (20260306):
- 1.6.1: Plugin install mechanism — RESOLVED. `claude plugin install` is marketplace-only (expects plugin-name@marketplace). Local plugins use `claude --plugin-dir ./path`. Our `hookwatch install` should: generate files + `bun link` + add to ~/.claude/settings.json + print instructions. `hookwatch uninstall` reverses this.
- 1.6.2: handler.ts vs binary in dev mode — RESOLVED. Run `bun link` first. Document in post-install output. Alternative: use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} in hook commands for dev mode portability.
- 1.6.3: ch-9eh removed from 1.6 — reparented as standalone release engineering issue.
- 1.6.4: hooks.json format confirmed — nested: { hooks: { EventType: [{ hooks: [{ type: "command", command: "hookwatch EventType" }] }] } }. Matcher optional (omit = catch all).
- 1.6.5: UI subcommand renamed from 'open' to 'ui'.
- 1.6.6: .claude-plugin/ directory contains ONLY plugin.json. hooks/ at plugin root.
- 1.6.7: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} env var available in hook commands — resolves to plugin directory absolute path.
- 1.6.8: package.json postinstall lifecycle hook must run `bun link` automatically. Scripts order: lifecycle commands first (postinstall, etc.), then other scripts in alphabetical order.
close_reason: Story 1.6 complete. CLI: 3 lowercase + 18 PascalCase subcommands, plugin generation, install/uninstall, bun link postinstall. Commit: 2c39de9

title: Research: hook execution environment — sandbox, shell interpreter, security implications for wrap
description:
Research how Claude Code executes hook commands. Claude Code source is not available — test empirically.

## Approach

Write shell-specific probe scripts that use syntax unique to each shell:
- POSIX sh probe: uses only POSIX syntax (control group)
- Bash probe: uses bash-only syntax (e.g., [[ ]], arrays, process substitution $(< file))
- Zsh probe: uses zsh-only syntax (e.g., glob qualifiers, =() process substitution)

Each probe writes its findings (which shell, env vars, sandbox info) to a known file so we can inspect results.

## Execution

Run probes as Claude Code hooks via 'claude --print' (headless/non-interactive mode). Use the Claude Code guide agent if unsure how to invoke headless mode. The probes should be registered as hooks and triggered by Claude Code.

## Output

1. Research report in docs/agents/qa/research-output/ with findings on:
   - Which shell interpreter Claude Code uses for hooks
   - Whether CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT, CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR, CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE are available
   - Any sandbox/isolation observations
   - Implications for hookwatch wrap command

2. Rerunnable probe scripts preserved alongside the report — NOT in tests/ (these are empirical probes, not software tests). Store in docs/agents/qa/research-output/shell-probes/

## Terminology

Call these 'probes' not 'tests' — tests verify our own code; probes investigate external behavior.
close_reason: Research complete. Findings: Claude Code uses sh -c for inline hook commands. macOS: /bin/sh (bash 3.2 POSIX mode). Shebangs respected for script files. Full probe report at docs/agents/qa/research-output/shell-probes-report.md. Cheat sheet drafted at docs/hook-execution-cheatsheet.md. Linux/Windows deferred — probe launcher is cross-platform for future runs.

description:

title: Architectural decision: persistent backend daemon (auto-started by handler)
description: hookwatch needs a persistent Bun daemon process. Handler auto-starts it on first hook invocation if not running. Daemon manages: SQLite writes, web UI server, WebSocket broadcast, wrap CLI. Self-terminates after inactivity. This resolves cold start performance (NFR-1), hook-server communication, and multi-plugin shared state. Update design doc with this decision.
notes: Idle timeout: daemon self-terminates after ~30-60 min of inactivity (no hook events, no web UI connections). Next hook invocation restarts it. No data loss — SQLite is the source of truth, not in-memory state. Use WAL mode for concurrent access.

title: Story 3.1: Wrap Command — Capture & Passthrough I/O
description:
As a hook author, I want to wrap any command to capture its stdin, stdout, and stderr, so that I can see exactly what data flows through my hook.

DESIGN (20260306 — supersedes earlier notes):

**No separate 'wrap' subcommand.** The 18 PascalCase event family subcommands (e.g. `hookwatch PreToolUse`) serve both modes:
- Bare handler: `hookwatch PreToolUse` — reads stdin, posts event, exits
- Wrapped: `hookwatch PreToolUse ./hook.sh` — spawns hook.sh, tees I/O, posts event
- Shell metacharacters: `hookwatch PreToolUse sh -c 'cmd1 | cmd2 && cmd3'`

**DB schema:** `wrapped_command` nullable TEXT column. NULL = bare handler, non-NULL = wrapping user's command. Same pipeline for both modes.

**Tee behavior:** Duplicate stdout and stderr to both the hookwatch server (for capture) and the terminal (for Claude Code to see). The event payload includes captured stdout/stderr.

**Exit code:** Forward child process exit code as hookwatch exit code.

**Best-effort capture:** If server unreachable after auto-start attempt, still pass through I/O (don't fail the wrapped command).
notes: Old children reference outdated 'wrap' subcommand design — need cleanup before dispatch.

title: Unified pipeline: merge bare+wrapped handlers with priority chain
description:
Merge runBare() and runWrappedMode() in src/handler/index.ts into a single unified function using the template method pattern.

Architecture (from ch-f3nl research):
- Single entry point. Branch on wrappedCommand being null/non-null.
- Bare mode = wrapped pipeline with no child process.
- Pipeline: readStdin -> parseEvent -> resolvePort -> [if wrappedCommand: spawn child] -> postEvent -> writeOutput

Error handling (priority chain from ch-cs6 decision):
- P1 Fatal (server unreachable, port occupied, POST fails): exit 2 + JSON in stdout. Always, both modes.
- P2 Non-fatal (server OK, hookwatch had an issue): log to hookwatch_error column in DB.
- P3 Normal: hookwatch_error NULL.
- Never exit 1 — useless (Claude Code shows generic error, stderr swallowed).
- Pass through wrapped command stdout/stderr/exit code unchanged (authenticity).

Implementation details:
- String builder for accumulating errors during handler run.
- POST hookwatch_error to DB at end of handler (non-fatal) or include in exit 2 JSON (fatal).
- Health check: POST response verification (non-cryptographic, async, in parallel with wrapped command).

Revealed by ch-f3nl research (closed). Decision documented in ch-cs6 (closed).
notes:
## Doubt resolutions (2026-03-07)

### Health check scope
Health check = verify POST response status inline. NOT a separate health endpoint (ch-6myr is separate and depends on ch-bh8e). No dependency on ch-6myr.

### Test updates
Update all exit 1 assertions to exit 2. Create a test helper that validates exit legality: exit code must be 0 or 2, and stdout must be valid JSON in the correct schema format for that exit code. Package as reusable helper in handler.test.ts.

### I/O logging (bare mode)
Log exactly what Claude Code sees: stdin/stdout/stderr as-is. Nothing more. Only exception: P1 fatal (POST fails / server unreachable) → exit 2 + JSON telling user to fix logging. Not blocked by ch-1qwj.

### File splitting
handler.test.ts may be large after updates — don't split now. Tracked in separate issue (threshold TBD).

title: docs: update hook-stdout-schema.md with correct exit code behavior
description:
## Problem

docs/hook-stdout-schema.md is incomplete and partially wrong about exit code behavior.
It says "Exit Code 0: stdout shown in transcript" / "Exit Code 2: stderr fed back to
Claude as error" — but misses the critical fact that JSON is ONLY parsed at exit 0.

## Changes needed

1. Add "Exit code behavior" section documenting all three exit codes:
   - Exit 0: JSON parsed, stdout processed
   - Exit 2: JSON IGNORED, stderr only, may block (per-event table)
   - Other: non-blocking, stderr in verbose mode
2. Add per-event blocking table (which events can block on exit 2)
3. Add "Decision control" section (JSON alternatives to exit 2)
4. Note upstream bug #30586 (JSON accidentally parsed at exit 2 — regression)
5. Link to official docs: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
6. Remove the misleading "Exit Code 0: stdout shown in transcript" line

## Signal exit code reference table

Add a reference table for 128+signal convention:

| Exit code | Signal  | Label                                    |
|-----------|---------|------------------------------------------|
| 129       | SIGHUP  | likely SIGHUP — terminal hangup          |
| 130       | SIGINT  | likely SIGINT — interrupted              |
| 131       | SIGQUIT | likely SIGQUIT — quit                    |
| 134       | SIGABRT | likely SIGABRT — aborted                 |
| 137       | SIGKILL | likely SIGKILL — forced termination      |
| 139       | SIGSEGV | likely SIGSEGV — segmentation fault      |
| 141       | SIGPIPE | likely SIGPIPE — broken pipe             |
| 143       | SIGTERM | likely SIGTERM — terminated              |

Note: "likely" because the 128+N convention is ambiguous — programs can
deliberately exit with codes >128, though this is rare in practice.

Note: macOS and Linux differ on some signal numbers (e.g. SIGBUS is 10 on
macOS, 7 on Linux).

## Source

Official Claude Code hooks reference: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
Sections: "Exit code output", "Exit code 2 behavior per event", "Decision control"
close_reason: Docs updated with exit code behavior, per-event blocking table, signal reference table. Commit: d08b169

title: Decide handler error surfacing strategy (exit 1 vs exit 0 + JSON error)
description: When handler fails to deliver event to server, how should it surface the error? Two options narrowed down: (A) Exit 1 + stderr message — simple but unclear if user sees it. (B) Exit 0 + JSON response with error field — Claude Code parses it but exit 0 implies success. Need to test actual Claude Code behavior with both approaches. Blocked by ch-20v (test non-JSON hook stdout). Only options A and B are acceptable.
notes:
## Decision (2026-03-07)

Strategy: priority chain with authenticity.

Priority 1 — Fatal (can't reach server): exit 2 + JSON always (both modes).
Priority 2 — Non-fatal (server OK): log to hookwatch_error DB column.
Priority 3 — Normal: hookwatch_error NULL.

Never exit 1 — empirically observed: Claude Code shows generic "hookname:subtype
hook error", stderr not surfaced. Strictly worse than exit 2.

Pass through wrapped command exit code unchanged — authenticity over
agent-friendliness. Reason: hook developers wrapping broken hooks must see real
failures, not false positives from agent-friendly rewrites.

New DB column: hookwatch_error TEXT (nullable) — stores hookwatch's own errors
for both bare and wrapped modes.

Implementation tracked in ch-f3nl (unified pipeline refactor).
close_reason: Decision made: priority chain (P1 exit 2 + JSON, P2 hookwatch_error column, P3 normal). Never exit 1. Pass through wrapped command exit code. Implementation in new issues from ch-f3nl research.

title: openAndInit: no error handling or rollback on DB version mismatch rename/recreate
description:
## Problem

src/db/connection.ts lines 64-85: the version mismatch branch calls renameSync,
new Database, chmodSync, applyFreshSchema without try-catch.

If renameSync succeeds but new Database or applyFreshSchema fails: original DB is
renamed away, new DB is in inconsistent state, no error message. Every subsequent
request fails AND the user loses their data.

If renameSync fails (EACCES, existing file): unhandled error propagates from openDb()
singleton, leaving db=null permanently.

## Fix

1. Rename old DB to `<name>.v<old_version>` (e.g. `hookwatch.db.v2`) instead of `.bak`.
   Version-based suffix avoids collision — each version gets a unique name.

2. If new Database or applyFreshSchema fails AFTER rename: do NOT try to rename back.
   Leave both files and show a clear [error] in hookwatch_log. Simpler and safer than
   rollback (which can also fail).

3. Wrap the rename/recreate block in try-catch. Surface errors clearly.

4. Add tests: point openAndInit() at a temp directory with a mismatched-version DB,
   verify rename happens and new DB is created. Test failure case too (e.g. read-only
   directory for new DB creation).

## Design decisions

- Rename suffix: `.v<version>` not `.bak` (version-specific, no collision)
- On recreate failure: leave both files + error log (no rollback attempt)
- Tests use existing temp dir sandbox (createHandlerTestContext pattern)

Sources: silent-failure-hunter (high #6)
close_reason: Backup suffix .v<version>, try-catch with error log, EISDIR failure test. Commit: 4d0c885

title: Research: unify bare handler and wrapped handler paths
description: runBare() and runWrappedMode() in src/handler/index.ts may have significant duplication: stdin reading, event parsing, port resolution, postEvent(), hook output writing, error handling. Research the two paths and report what's shared, what's different, and how they could be unified. Output: research report with specific refactoring recommendations. Do NOT write code — research only.
notes:
## Design Decisions (2026-03-07)

### Architecture: Unified pipeline (template method)

Single entry point. wrappedCommand being null/non-null is the branch condition.
Bare mode = wrapped pipeline with no child process.

Pipeline: readStdin → parseEvent → resolvePort → [if wrappedCommand: spawn child] → postEvent → writeOutput

Template method pattern without inheritance — one algorithm skeleton with a
conditional branch. Replaces strategy pattern (two separate functions sharing
80%+ of logic).

### Error handling: priority chain

P1 — Fatal (server unreachable, port occupied, POST fails):
  Exit 2 + JSON in stdout. Always. Both bare and wrapped modes.
  User must fix hookwatch first.

P2 — Non-fatal (server reachable, hookwatch had an issue):
  Log to hookwatch_error column in DB. If we can POST, we can write to DB.
  No separate log file fallback needed.

P3 — Normal (no hookwatch error):
  hookwatch_error is NULL. Normal operation.

New DB column: hookwatch_error TEXT (nullable).

### Exit code strategy

- Never exit 1 — generic "hook error", stderr swallowed. Useless.
- Exit 2 + JSON for fatal hookwatch errors (both modes)
- Pass through wrapped command's stdout, stderr, exit code unchanged

### Why authenticity over agent-friendliness

A hook developer wrapping their broken hook with hookwatch wrap must see the
real failure. If hookwatch rewrites exit 1 to exit 2, the developer sees a clean
exit — a false positive during debugging. hookwatch observes; it does not heal.

### Health check verification

POST response verification (non-cryptographic) can run async, in parallel with
the wrapped command. Health probe confirms server responsiveness.
close_reason: Research complete. Design decisions documented in notes: unified pipeline (template method), priority chain, hookwatch_error column, exit 2 strategy. Implementation split into new issues.

title: Structural type safety: branded types or class-based validation at boundaries
description:
Smart constructor pattern (Option C) at every trust boundary. Each boundary gets a dedicated parse function that validates shape and returns a typed result or throws with context. Eliminates scattered isRecord() calls and as casts.

Boundaries:
1. stdin → HookEvent: DONE (Zod parseHookEvent)
2. subprocess stdout → HookOutputJson: needs parse function (currently parseStdout with unvalidated cast)
3. bun:sqlite → EventRow: stays as-is (trusted internal boundary, own schema)
4. SSE/fetch → event data: needs parse function (currently isRecord guard)

Design: parse functions compose naturally with Zod (which returns plain objects). Class-based approach (Option D) rejected — adds this-binding friction and double-construction overhead with Zod. instanceof runtime identity not needed since Zod is the validation gate.

Related: review findings #1 (isRecord accepts arrays — band-aid applied), #2 (dead stringField guard — removed), #4 (parseStdout error context — band-aid applied).
notes:
Design decision: Factory Method pattern with Zod inside at every trust boundary.

Scope:
- Boundary #2 (subprocess stdout): parseStdout → factory with Zod schema for hook output JSON
- Boundary #4 (SSE/fetch): isRecord() calls in ingest.ts, event-detail.ts, event-list.ts, sse-client.ts → factory functions with Zod

Removals:
- stringField() — deleted entirely
- isRecord() — no longer public export. Inline into parseHookEvent() or keep as private helper in that file only.

Pattern: same as existing parseHookEvent(). Raw input → Zod validate → typed output or throw. No direct schema.parse() in consuming code.

Interim band-aids in place (commit c138b1f): isRecord excludes arrays, parseStdout wraps JSON.parse with error context, dead stringField guard removed. These get superseded by the factory work.
close_reason: Factory Method pattern with Zod at all trust boundaries. parseHookOutput (boundary #2), parseSseEvent (boundary #4). isRecord removed from public API. stringField deleted.

title: Test infra: empty catches swallow parse errors in test-server.ts and subprocess.ts
description:
Two empty catch blocks in shared test utilities silently swallow JSON parse errors, making test failures misleading.

1. src/test/test-server.ts lines 53-57: req.json() failure sets body=null — indistinguishable from legitimate null body. Tests checking server.events[0].body see null and pass/fail for wrong reason.

title: wrap.ts exitCode ?? 1 uses forbidden exit code on signal kill [needs-design]
description:
## Problem

src/handler/wrap.ts line 88: exitCode: exitCode ?? 1 — when child process is killed
by signal (SIGKILL, SIGSEGV), Bun returns null for exit code. The null-to-1 conversion
uses the forbidden exit code 1 which causes Claude Code to show generic 'hook error'.

Project decision: never exit 1 (strictly useless). Yet signal-killed children silently
become exit 1.

## Fix

1. Convert signal deaths to 128+signal convention (e.g. SIGKILL → 137, SIGTERM → 143).
   This is the universal convention (bash, Docker, Kubernetes all use it). Exit codes
   129-255 fall into Claude Code's "any other exit code = non-blocking" category.

2. Add signal mapping utility (src/handler/signals.ts or src/handler/errors.ts, ~20 lines):
   - Map of signal name → number for macOS signals
   - Function: signalExitCode(signal: string | null): number — returns 128+N or 1 as fallback
   - Function: describeExitCode(code: number): string | null — returns human-readable label
     e.g. 137 → "likely SIGKILL — forced termination"

3. Log signal deaths in hookwatch_log:
   [warn] exit 137 (likely SIGKILL — forced termination)

4. Realistic signals for hookwatch context:
   - 137 SIGKILL — OOM killer, kill -9, Claude Code timeout
   - 143 SIGTERM — graceful shutdown, Claude Code stopping hooks
   - 130 SIGINT — user pressed Ctrl+C
   - 134 SIGABRT — Bun assertion failure
   - 139 SIGSEGV — native code crash
   - 141 SIGPIPE — server closed connection while writing

5. Use "likely" qualifier because the 128+N convention is ambiguous — a program
   could deliberately exit(137), though this is extremely rare in practice.

## Design decisions

- Always exit 0 for hookwatch's own errors (ch-wfvs handles this)
- For wrapped commands: forward 128+signal as exit code to Claude Code (non-blocking)
- Store 128+signal in DB exit_code column (NOT NULL INTEGER)
- Add signal context to hookwatch_log when available from Bun's signal field

## References

- POSIX 128+signal convention: universal (bash $?, Docker, Kubernetes)
- Ambiguity acknowledged: programs CAN exit >128 deliberately (IBM LSF docs, Mocha)
- macOS signal numbers differ from Linux for some signals (SIGBUS=10 vs 7)
notes: Research complete (ch-4ig3 closed). Signal-kill behavior fully documented: SIGKILL/SIGTERM/exit-1 all produce identical non-blocking 'hook error' from Claude Code. Design settled: 128+signal convention. Ready for implementation.

title: readPort/readPortFileSync: invalid port value silently falls back with no warning
description:
Two functions do the exact same logic (read port file → parse int → validate range → handle errors). Historical accident — written by different agents for different stories.

1. src/handler/index.ts readPort() lines 84-106: returns { port, warning }. Used by handler POST path. Has console.error for invalid value BUT returns warning:null (bug).

2. src/handler/spawn.ts readPortFileSync() lines 36-51: returns just number. Used by waitForHealth() during auto-start polling. Silent fallback on invalid value — no logging at all.

Fix: Consolidate into ONE function in src/paths.ts (already owns portFilePath()). Returns { port: number, warning: string | null }. Invalid/corrupt port → warning string + console.error. ENOENT → silent fallback (expected). Other OS errors (EACCES, EIO) → warning string + console.error. Callers that only need the port destructure { port } and ignore warning.

Remove both original functions after consolidation.

Sources: silent-failure-hunter (high #3, #4), quality report (port reading consolidation)

title: Component version alignment: shared version constant + API version header
description:
All hookwatch components should declare their version for alignment verification.

Motivation: detect stale server processes (server started with v2, handler updated to v3). Not security — alignment.

1. Create src/version.ts with a single exported VERSION constant (semver string from package.json or hardcoded).
2. Server: return X-Hookwatch-Version header on all API responses (POST /events, GET /events, GET /sessions, health check).
3. Handler: check X-Hookwatch-Version in POST response. If mismatch with own VERSION, log [warn] to hookwatch_log.
4. CLI: --version/-v already works via citty. Ensure it reads from the same VERSION constant.
5. DB: PRAGMA user_version is the schema version (integer), not the component version (semver). They are separate concepts — schema version tracks table structure, component version tracks code compatibility.

Non-cryptographic verification (future): a shared build token or hash to confirm components are from the same build. Not needed for v0 — version string comparison is sufficient. Track as a follow-up if version drift becomes a real problem.

Agent: TBD (touches handler, server, cli — may need splitting).

title: Story 2.4a: SSE endpoint + broadcast (server)
description:
Server-side SSE implementation. Agent: server.

## Files to create/modify
- src/server/stream.ts — SSE endpoint handler for GET /api/events/stream. Maintains a Set of connected clients (ReadableStream controllers). Sends `data: {json}\n\n` format. Handles client disconnection (remove from Set).
- src/server/ingest.ts — After successful event insertion into SQLite, call broadcast(event) to push to all SSE clients.
- src/server/index.ts — Register GET /api/events/stream route to stream handler.

## Bun SSE pattern
```ts
new Response(new ReadableStream({
  start(controller) { clients.add(controller); },
  cancel() { clients.delete(controller); }
}), { headers: { "Content-Type": "text/event-stream", "Cache-Control": "no-cache" } })
```

## Key constraint
- Broadcast ALL events to ALL clients — session filtering happens client-side (Story 2.4b)
close_reason: Implemented in Story 2.4a merge. Commit: 64f5fd4

title: Story 2.4b: EventSource client + signal wiring (ui)
description:
Client-side SSE consumer. Agent: ui. Depends on 2.4a (needs the SSE endpoint).

## Files to create/modify
- src/ui/shared/sse-client.ts — Create EventSource connected to /api/events/stream. Parse incoming events. Push to eventList signal. EventSource auto-reconnects by default — no custom reconnect logic needed.
- src/ui/app.ts or event-list.ts — Wire SSE client on mount. Insert new events at the BEGINNING of eventList array (maintains reverse-chronological order without re-fetching).

## Session filter check
- Before adding an SSE event to eventList, check if activeSession signal is set
- If set and event.session_id doesn't match, skip it (don't add to list)
- If null (all sessions), always add

title: QA: verify hook commands use POSIX sh — bash/zsh syntax may fail
description:
Claude Code docs say hooks run as "shell commands" but do not specify which shell interpreter. Verify:

1. Test a hook command using bash-specific syntax (e.g., [[ ]] double brackets, process substitution <(), arrays) — does it work or fail?
2. Test a hook command using zsh-specific syntax (e.g., glob qualifiers, ${(s.:.)var} splitting) — does it work or fail?
3. Test a plain POSIX sh command — confirm it works.
4. Check if Claude Code uses /bin/sh, /bin/bash, or the users default shell to execute hook commands.

If bash/zsh syntax works, document it. If only POSIX sh works, document that hookwatch wrap must spawn hooks with /bin/sh (not bash/zsh) to match Claude Code behavior.

Related: ch-5uod (hook execution environment research).
close_reason: Resolved by ch-5uod inline probes. Claude Code uses sh -c for inline hook commands. POSIX sh only — bash/zsh syntax fails. Documented in docs/hook-execution-cheatsheet.md.

title: refactor: exitFatal to exit 0 + JSON systemMessage (never exit 2)
description:
## Problem

exitFatal() uses exit 2 + stdout JSON, but Claude Code docs state:
- "JSON output is only processed on exit 0"
- "Exit 2: Claude Code ignores stdout and any JSON in it"
- Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks (section "Exit code output")

Our exitFatal design is broken: the JSON containing hookwatch_fatal, continue:true,
and systemMessage is silently discarded by Claude Code at exit 2. It accidentally
works due to bug #30586 (regression — JSON parsed at exit 2), but we cannot rely
on buggy behavior.

Additionally, exit 2 blocks PreToolUse/UserPromptSubmit events — violating our
passive observer principle ("never block Claude Code").

## Fix

Change exitFatal() to:
- Exit 0 (not 2)
- JSON stdout with systemMessage containing the error message
- continue: true (default at exit 0, but explicit for clarity)
- Keep hookwatch_fatal field for hookwatch's own records

After this change, hookwatch can NEVER block Claude Code:
- Deliberate errors → exit 0 + JSON (non-blocking, error visible via systemMessage)
- Uncontrolled crashes → exit 137/139/etc (non-blocking per docs)

## Scope

- src/handler/index.ts: exitFatal() function (lines 51-65)
- src/handler/index.ts: JSDoc comments referencing exit 2
- All test files asserting exit code 2 for fatal scenarios
- Update error strategy comments in handleHook()

## References

- Claude Code docs: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
- Upstream bug (JSON parsed at exit 2): https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/30586
- Exit 2 not blocking Task/Bash: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/26923
close_reason: exitFatal now exits 0 + JSON systemMessage. All tests updated (exit 2 → exit 0). Commit: a42fccb

title: Migrate error handling from try-catch to Rust-style Result returns
description:
Replace try-catch error handling with Rust-style Result<T, E> pattern throughout the handler. Errors should be returned as data (discriminated unions) instead of thrown and caught.

This is a systematic refactor affecting:
- postEvent() and its internal spawn/retry logic
- handleHook() error paths
- Any other try-catch blocks in the handler

Pattern: { ok: true, value: T } | { ok: false, error: E } instead of try { ... } catch (e) { ... }

PostEventResult (ch-6k4y) is already a step in this direction. Extend the pattern to all error boundaries.

Scope: v1 hardening (after v0 release).
